---
title: "pU-QTL mapping — binomial GLM model"
author: "XSun"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  workflowr::wflow_html:
    code_folding: hide
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.align = "center", fig.width = 8, fig.height = 5)
```

# Overview

We map genetic effects on pseudouridylation (%pU) with the **simplest**
count-based model as a starting point: model the raw converted-read counts
as **binomial**, test the genotype effect on the log-odds of the
modification rate, and assess significance with a **permutation LOD
threshold**. This deliberately omits overdispersion (beta-binomial) and
relatedness (mixed model) for now — those are natural extensions once the
simple model is in place.

Sites tested: the **5-fold union (called in ≥ 1 fold)** set,
**`r format(12662, big.mark=",")` sites** (see the 5-fold site-calling
page). Samples: the **50 genotyped cell lines**.

# The model

For a given pU site, let individual $i$ have $y_i$ converted reads out of
$n_i$ total reads ($n_i = C_i + T_i$, the read depth at that site):

$$
y_i \mid p_i \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(n_i,\, p_i)
$$
$$
\text{logit}(p_i) \;=\; \beta_0 \;+\; \beta_g\, G_i \;+\; z_i^\top \gamma
$$

- $p_i$ — the site's true modification rate in individual $i$.
- $n_i$ — read depth; enters as the binomial trial count, so **low-depth
  observations automatically carry less weight**.
- $G_i \in \{0,1,2\}$ — genotype of the **test SNP** (a cis variant near the
  site).
- $\beta_g$ — the **genetic effect we test**.
- $z_i$ — covariates (e.g. genotype PCs to correct population structure);
  optional in this first pass.

This is a standard binomial GLM (logit link). It is the simplest member of
the count-model family — no overdispersion parameter, no random effects.

# QTL mapping via LOD

For each (site, cis-SNP) pair:

1. Fit the **full** model $\text{logit}(p_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_g G_i (+ z_i^\top\gamma)$
   and the **null** model with $\beta_g = 0$.
2. Compute the **LOD score**:

$$
\text{LOD} \;=\; \frac{\log\text{Lik}_{\text{full}} - \log\text{Lik}_{\text{null}}}{\log(10)}
$$

Higher LOD = stronger evidence that the test SNP affects %pU at that site.
For a site with several cis SNPs, the site's signal is the maximum LOD over
its SNPs.

# Significance: permutation threshold

A genome-wide, multiple-testing-aware threshold (Churchill & Doerge 1994):

1. **Permute** the phenotype-to-genotype assignment across individuals
   (breaks any real genotype-phenotype relationship), ~**1000×**.
2. For each permutation, record the **genome-wide maximum LOD** across all
   (site, SNP) tests.
3. The **95th percentile** of those 1000 maxima is the genome-wide 5%
   significance threshold.
4. Sites/SNPs whose observed LOD exceeds the threshold are **candidate
   QTL**.

<!-- # Illustrative example (simulated) -->

<!-- A small self-contained simulation of one site's cis-scan, to show the -->
<!-- mechanics concretely. 50 individuals, one site, 50 candidate cis-SNPs, where -->
<!-- only SNP #1 has a true effect. -->

<!-- ```{r sim-setup} -->
<!-- set.seed(1) -->
<!-- n   <- 50            # individuals -->
<!-- M   <- 50            # candidate cis SNPs for this site -->
<!-- depth <- rpois(n, 60) + 10                 # per-individual read depth n_i -->
<!-- G   <- matrix(rbinom(n * M, 2, 0.30), nrow = n)   # genotypes (n x M), MAF ~ 0.3 -->

<!-- # true phenotype: only SNP #1 affects %pU -->
<!-- beta0 <- -1.5; beta_g <- 0.8 -->
<!-- p  <- plogis(beta0 + beta_g * G[, 1]) -->
<!-- y  <- rbinom(n, depth, p)                  # converted reads y_i -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("n = %d individuals, %d candidate SNPs; mean depth = %.0f\n", n, M, mean(depth))) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r lod-fn} -->
<!-- # null log-lik (intercept only) is invariant to individual order -->
<!-- ll_null <- as.numeric(logLik(glm(cbind(y, depth - y) ~ 1, family = binomial))) -->

<!-- lod_snp <- function(g, y, depth, ll0) { -->
<!--   f <- suppressWarnings(glm(cbind(y, depth - y) ~ g, family = binomial)) -->
<!--   (as.numeric(logLik(f)) - ll0) / log(10) -->
<!-- } -->

<!-- obs_lod <- apply(G, 2, lod_snp, y = y, depth = depth, ll0 = ll_null) -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("Observed LOD: SNP#1 (true) = %.2f ; max of the other %d = %.2f\n", -->
<!--             obs_lod[1], M - 1, max(obs_lod[-1]))) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r permutation} -->
<!-- set.seed(2) -->
<!-- n_perm <- 500                              # (1000 in the real analysis) -->
<!-- perm_max <- replicate(n_perm, { -->
<!--   idx <- sample(n)                         # permute phenotype vs genotype -->
<!--   yp <- y[idx]; dp <- depth[idx] -->
<!--   ll0p <- as.numeric(logLik(glm(cbind(yp, dp - yp) ~ 1, family = binomial))) -->
<!--   max(apply(G, 2, lod_snp, y = yp, depth = dp, ll0 = ll0p))   # genome-wide max -->
<!-- }) -->
<!-- thr <- quantile(perm_max, 0.95) -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("95%% permutation LOD threshold = %.2f\n", thr)) -->
<!-- cat(sprintf("SNP#1 observed LOD (%.2f) exceeds threshold: %s\n", -->
<!--             obs_lod[1], obs_lod[1] > thr)) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r sim-plot, fig.height=4.5} -->
<!-- dt <- data.table(snp = seq_len(M), lod = obs_lod, is_causal = c(TRUE, rep(FALSE, M - 1))) -->
<!-- ggplot(dt, aes(x = snp, y = lod, color = is_causal)) + -->
<!--   geom_point(size = 2) + -->
<!--   geom_hline(yintercept = thr, linetype = "dashed", color = "firebrick") + -->
<!--   annotate("text", x = M * 0.7, y = thr, vjust = -0.5, color = "firebrick", -->
<!--            label = "95% permutation threshold") + -->
<!--   scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "firebrick", `FALSE` = "grey50"), -->
<!--                      labels = c(`TRUE` = "true causal SNP", `FALSE` = "null SNP"), name = NULL) + -->
<!--   labs(x = "candidate cis-SNP", y = "LOD", -->
<!--        title = "Illustrative single-site cis-scan: LOD per SNP vs. permutation threshold") + -->
<!--   theme_bw(base_size = 13) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- ```{r perm-null, fig.height=4} -->
<!-- ggplot(data.table(perm_max = perm_max), aes(x = perm_max)) + -->
<!--   geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue") + -->
<!--   geom_vline(xintercept = thr, linetype = "dashed", color = "firebrick") + -->
<!--   labs(x = "per-permutation genome-wide max LOD", y = "count", -->
<!--        title = "Permutation null of the (genome-wide max) LOD; dashed = 95th percentile") + -->
<!--   theme_bw(base_size = 13) -->
<!-- ``` -->

<!-- The causal SNP's LOD sits well above the permutation threshold, while the -->
<!-- null SNPs stay below it — the procedure recovers the true signal and -->
<!-- controls the genome-wide error rate. -->

# Data 

- **Sites**: 5-fold union, ≥ 1 fold — `r format(12662, big.mark=",")` pU
  sites. (A stricter ≥ k-fold set could be substituted; see the 5-fold
  page https://xsun1229.github.io/pumseq/mRNA_5fold_union_site_calling.html.)
- **Phenotype**: per site, per cell line — converted reads $C$ (= $y$) out
  of total depth $N$ (= $C+T$), from the pooled joint counts.
- **Genotypes**: cis SNPs within a window of each site (e.g. ±5kb, 20kb, 50kb, 100kb), from
  the imputed genotypes of the 50 cell lines.
- **Covariates $z$**: optional in this first pass (candidate: phenotype PCs).

<!-- # Notes -->

<!-- - **Why start here**: the binomial GLM is fast (a plain `glm` per -->
<!--   (site, SNP)), so a genome-wide scan with 1000 permutations is -->
<!--   computationally feasible — unlike a mixed model. -->
<!-- - **Planned extensions** (once the simple model is validated): overdispersion -->
<!--   via a beta-binomial / observation-level random effect; the -->
<!--   `treated − input` contrast via an input offset or a treatment×genotype -->
<!--   interaction; relatedness via a random effect or genotype-PC covariates. -->
<!-- - The example above is simulated purely to illustrate the LOD + permutation -->
<!--   mechanics; the genome-wide run on the real sites is the next step. -->
